critic_raw:
PCA is applied to 'reduce the dimensionality of genomic information,' fitted on training data only. For genomic sequences (presumably gene expression arrays or mutation profiles), PCA computed on training patients only will have a different principal component space than PCA computed on the full population. When test-set patients are projected into this training-derived PC space, their projections are valid only if the training set is representative of the full genomic diversity. With 5,000 patients and potentially tens of thousands of genomic features, PCA on the training split alone may capture training-set-specific genomic variation (from ancestry stratification or cohort enrollment biases) that does not represent the test population. This is a real and documented problem in genomic ML (population stratification confounding PCA).

Accuracy is the primary metric for a four-class chemotherapy response prediction problem. Chemotherapy response classification has well-documented class imbalance (pathological complete response, the most favorable outcome, is rare in most subtypes). Accuracy will be dominated by the majority class (partial or no response), and a model that classifies all patients into the two majority classes achieves high accuracy without providing clinically useful discrimination of complete responders. Macro F1-score would be more appropriate and is listed only as secondary.

The multi-modal model combines 'feedforward and convolutional networks.' CNN on what input? Genomic data is typically tabular (gene expression values, mutation presence/absence) or sequence data (nucleotide sequences). CNNs are appropriate for 2D images or 1D sequence data, but applying a CNN to a tabular genomic feature vector is architecturally unusual and requires justification. If the genomic input is processed as a 1D sequence through a CNN, the positional encoding implies adjacent genes have correlated expression in a biologically meaningful way—which is not generally true for gene expression arrays ordered by chromosome position after PCA.

The clinicopathological-only logistic regression baseline does not isolate the contribution of genomic features from architectural complexity. To test whether genomic data adds value, the comparison must be multi-modal model vs. clinicopathological-plus-genomic logistic regression. By comparing against clinicopathological-only logistic regression, the observed improvement conflates the information gain from genomics with the nonlinear representational power of the deep learning architecture.

Patient follow-up variability is described as a confound managed by including follow-up duration as a covariate. However, if follow-up is short, some chemotherapy responses cannot yet be classified—the response classification itself is right-censored. A patient with 6-month follow-up cannot be reliably classified as a complete vs. partial responder if response assessment requires 12 months of observation. The design does not describe how minimum follow-up requirements are enforced, meaning some labels may be based on incomplete observation and systematically misclassified.
all_issues_raised: PCA for genomic dimensionality reduction computed on the training split is vulnerable to population stratification confounding: training-set-specific ancestry or enrollment patterns define the PC space, and test patients projected into this space may have distorted representations.

Accuracy as the primary metric for four-class chemotherapy response prediction is dominated by majority-class outcomes; a model classifying all patients into the two most common response categories achieves high accuracy while failing to identify complete responders.

Applying a CNN to genomic data is architecturally unusual; if the genomic input is treated as a 1D sequence, the positional encoding implies biologically meaningful adjacency between genes that does not hold for expression arrays ordered by chromosome.

The comparison conflates architectural complexity with information gain: comparing multi-modal deep learning (clinicopathological + genomic) to clinicopathological-only logistic regression does not isolate whether genomic data adds value beyond richer modeling.

Chemotherapy response labels require minimum follow-up duration that is not enforced; patients with short follow-up have incomplete observation windows and may be systematically misclassified, contaminating the training signal.
all_issues_adjudicated: PCA for genomic dimensionality reduction computed on the training split is vulnerable to population stratification confounding: training-set-specific ancestry or enrollment patterns define the PC space, and test patients projected into this space may have distorted representations.

Accuracy as the primary metric for four-class chemotherapy response prediction is dominated by majority-class outcomes; a model classifying all patients into the two most common response categories achieves high accuracy while failing to identify complete responders.

Applying a CNN to genomic data is architecturally unusual; if the genomic input is treated as a 1D sequence, the positional encoding implies biologically meaningful adjacency between genes that does not hold for expression arrays ordered by chromosome.

The comparison conflates architectural complexity with information gain: comparing multi-modal deep learning (clinicopathological + genomic) to clinicopathological-only logistic regression does not isolate whether genomic data adds value beyond richer modeling.

Chemotherapy response labels require minimum follow-up duration that is not enforced; patients with short follow-up have incomplete observation windows and may be systematically misclassified, contaminating the training signal.
